title: Nahum
author: Nahum the Elkoshite (location unknown)
date: ~663–612 BC (after the fall of Thebes and before Nineveh's fall)
setting: Nineveh, the capital of the brutal Assyrian empire, is now the target of God's judgment — about 150 years after Jonah's mission there.
purpose: To announce the certain fall of Nineveh as a comfort to Judah suffering under Assyrian cruelty, and to demonstrate that God's long-delayed judgment on arrogant oppressors will surely come.
themes: God as refuge for those who trust him
The fall of the proud — Nineveh as a case study
Comfort for the oppressed: your enemy will be destroyed

outline.label: God's Character: Jealous, Avenging, Patient — but Just

before.label: Jonah preaches at Nineveh; the city repents temporarily
Sennacherib's siege of Jerusalem; Assyria at its height
Fall of Thebes (No-Amon) to Assyria — cited as recent past

after.label: Nineveh falls to Babylon and the Medes
Habakkuk — Babylonian rise and the problem of theodicy
Battle of Carchemish — Babylon becomes the world power

key_people.name: Nahum of Elkosh
key_people.role: The prophet; his name means 'comfort' — comfort, in this case, for Judah in the form of Nineveh's complete and permanent destruction

context: Nahum prophesied between 663 BC (the fall of Thebes, cited as past in 3:8) and 612 BC (Nineveh's actual fall). He addresses the Assyrian empire at the height of its power — the empire that had destroyed Israel in 722 BC and devastated Judah under Hezekiah. Where Jonah announced Nineveh's potential destruction and saw repentance delay it, Nahum announces its certain destruction a century and a half later, after Nineveh has reverted to its characteristic cruelty.
christ_connection: Nahum 1:15 ('Behold, upon the mountains, the feet of him who brings good news, who publishes peace!') is echoed in Isaiah 52:7 and applied in Romans 10:15 to the preaching of the gospel. The certainty of God's judgment against those who crush the weak is the necessary backdrop to the gospel's good news: the justice that Nineveh receives in full measure is the justice that Christ received in the place of his people. The book demonstrates that God's patience with sin has a limit and that covenant faithfulness includes the destruction of those who destroy.

key_verses.note: 'The LORD is slow to anger and great in power, and the LORD will by no means clear the guilty' — the standard covenant formula of Exod 34:6–7, but with the merciful elements stripped away to emphasize the justice

'The LORD is good, a stronghold in the day of trouble; he knows those who take refuge in him' — the pastoral anchor within an oracle of judgment: God's goodness is precisely what his judgment of evil demonstrates

themes_detail.title: God's Patient Justice and Its Limits
themes_detail.text: Nahum begins with a theological poem (1:2–8) that draws directly on the covenant formula of Exodus 34:6–7, but deliberately emphasizes the justice side: God is jealous, avenging, wrathful — and by no means clears the guilty. This is not in tension with God's mercy but coordinates with it: the same God who was patient enough to spare Nineveh at Jonah's preaching is now, after a century and a half of resumed cruelty, bringing final judgment. God's patience is not unlimited, and Nahum announces that Nineveh's limit has been reached.

themes_detail.title: The Destruction of the Oppressor as Good News
themes_detail.text: Nahum presents Nineveh's destruction as gospel — literally good news (1:15). This seems offensive to modern readers accustomed to thinking of judgment as incompatible with love, but Nahum represents the perspective of those crushed by Nineveh's empire. The end of Nineveh is relief, liberation, and restoration for the nations it had devastated. The book is a sustained argument that God's justice — including the destruction of those who destroy — is part of his goodness, not a contradiction of it. The poor and oppressed have a stake in God's judgment.

themes_detail.title: The Certainty of Prophesied Judgment
themes_detail.text: Nahum's oracle is entirely predictive — Nineveh has not yet fallen when he writes, and the book makes no call to repentance, no offer of conditional escape. This unconditional certainty is itself a theological statement: there are moments in history when God's patience is exhausted and judgment is no longer conditional. The fall of Nineveh in 612 BC was so complete and so sudden (the city was never rebuilt as a major centre) that it served for centuries as a demonstration that prophetic words are reliable and that no empire is indestructible.

themes_detail.title: The Character of God as the Foundation of Hope
themes_detail.text: Within the oracle of judgment, Nahum 1:7 inserts a word of pastoral assurance: 'The LORD is good, a stronghold in the day of trouble; he knows those who take refuge in him.' This verse is not incidental but structural: the same God whose judgment falls on Nineveh is a refuge for those who trust him. God's goodness and God's wrath are not contradictions; they are the same attribute exercised toward different objects. For the nations crushed by Nineveh, his wrath against the oppressor is the form his goodness takes on their behalf.
